Case title: LLM-assisted grant proposal ideation in competitive funding calls
Short description: Compare AI-assisted and non-assisted proposal ideation under real funding calls to test whether AI changes what gets proposed, not just how it is written.
Empirical unit of analysis: Proposal abstracts, aims pages, ideation memos, and AI-use traces
Observable human actors: PI, funder-facing applicant, postdoc, doctoral student
Candidate roles tested: Relevance adjudicator; accountability and labor allocator
Key typology question: Does AI compress agendas toward institutionally legible questions?
Observable artifacts / traces: Draft histories, semantic-distinctiveness scores, reviewer comments, AI-use logs
Key outcomes observed: Novelty, semantic distinctiveness, stakeholder relevance, funding outcomes
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: AI use leaves distinctiveness and stakeholder relevance unchanged or improves both
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Austerity, field maturity, and call design can compress agendas even without AI
Why this case has high leverage: Without a funding case, relevance adjudication remains underspecified and too abstract.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
